questions.question: What is the unit of bytecode produced by javac for a Java source file containing three top-level classes?
choices: One .class file containing all three classes.
Three .class files, one per top-level class.
One assembly (.dll) listing all three classes.
questions.explanation: javac emits one .class file per top-level class. JARs aggregate .class files for deployment but each top-level class is still its own .class - unlike .NET where types are bundled into an assembly.

questions.question: You measure a Java method's runtime by invoking it once and printing System.nanoTime() deltas. The result is much slower than the equivalent .NET method. What is the most likely flaw?
choices: Java methods are inherently slower for single invocations and the result is correct.
HotSpot's two-tier JIT hasn't promoted the method yet; you measured the interpreter.
javac inserts debug instrumentation that dominates a single call.
The classpath was misconfigured and a slower fallback class was loaded.
questions.explanation: HotSpot interprets cold code, then promotes to C1 (~1,500 invocations) and later C2. A single invocation never reaches the optimized tiers, so the measurement reflects the interpreter, not steady-state performance.

questions.question: You want to ship a Spring Boot service as a single native binary with no JVM dependency. Which option matches and what is the main trade-off compared to .NET's NativeAOT?
choices: Use jlink - it bundles the JVM and bytecode and is equivalent to NativeAOT.
Use GraalVM Native Image - it produces a native binary but reflection and dynamic class loading require explicit configuration.
Use the standard java launcher - Java has produced native binaries by default since Java 17.
Use Maven's shade plugin - it embeds the JVM into a fat JAR.
questions.explanation: GraalVM Native Image is Java's AOT-to-native answer. Unlike .NET NativeAOT, which has tighter first-party tooling, GraalVM historically required explicit metadata for reflection and resources - Spring Boot 3's AOT support has made this practical but not invisible.
